title: Infections of the Skin Muscles and Soft Tissues
authors: Dennis L. Stevens, Amy E. Bryant

items.subtopic: Infections of the Skin, Muscles, and Soft Tissues
items.title: Why are skin and soft tissue infections becoming more frequent and severe?
items.content: The increase in frequency and severity of skin and soft tissue infections is attributed to several factors. Efficient global air travel facilitates rapid microbial dissemination, leading to increased virulence and antibiotic resistance. Natural disasters such as earthquakes and hurricanes cause injuries that predispose individuals to infection. Additionally, trauma from combat and terrorist activities can damage tissues, allowing pathogens easy access to deeper structures.
keyPoints: Microbes are rapidly disseminated worldwide via air travel, acquiring genes for virulence factors and antibiotic resistance.
Natural disasters like earthquakes, tsunamis, tornadoes, and hurricanes increase in frequency, causing skin and soft tissue damage that predisposes to infection.
Trauma from combat and terrorist activities can severely damage tissues, providing pathogens with access to deeper structures.
items.reference: Infections of the Skin Muscles and Soft Tissues · Infections of the Skin, Muscles, and Soft Tissues

items.subtopic: Infections of the Skin, Muscles, and Soft Tissues
items.title: How do natural disasters and human-instigated events impact skin and soft tissue infections?
items.content: Natural disasters such as earthquakes and hurricanes increase in frequency and cause injuries that predispose individuals to severe skin and soft tissue infections. Human-instigated events like combat and terrorist activities also result in significant trauma, allowing pathogens access to deeper tissues. The lack of immediate medical intervention during these events can delay treatment, increasing the risk of severe infection and death.
keyPoints: Injuries from natural disasters predispose individuals to skin and soft tissue infections due to major damage.
Combat and terrorist activities cause trauma that allows pathogens access to deeper tissues.
Delayed primary treatment during disasters increases the likelihood of severe infection and death.
items.reference: Infections of the Skin Muscles and Soft Tissues · Infections of the Skin, Muscles, and Soft Tissues

items.subtopic: ANATOMIC RELATIONSHIPS: CLUES TO THE DIAGNOSIS OF SOFT TISSUE INFECTIONS (part 1)
items.title: Mechanisms of Epidermal Infection and Spread
items.content: The epidermis is protected by the stratum corneum, which acts as a mechanical barrier. Disruption through various means allows bacterial penetration to deeper structures. Bacteria like Streptococcus pyogenes can spread laterally via lymphatics, causing rapid superficial spread of infections such as erysipelas.
keyPoints: The epidermis is protected by the stratum corneum and lacks blood vessels.
Disruption allows bacterial penetration to deeper structures.
Streptococcus pyogenes can cause lateral translocation via lymphatics, leading to erysipelas.
items.reference: Infections of the Skin Muscles and Soft Tissues · ANATOMIC RELATIONSHIPS: CLUES TO THE DIAGNOSIS OF SOFT TISSUE INFECTIONS (part 1)

items.subtopic: ANATOMIC RELATIONSHIPS: CLUES TO THE DIAGNOSIS OF SOFT TISSUE INFECTIONS (part 1)
items.title: Clinical Signs of Infective Vasculitis
items.content: Infective vasculitis of the capillary plexus beneath dermal papillae can manifest as petechiae, Osler nodes, Janeway lesions, and palpable purpura. These signs are crucial clues for diagnosing endocarditis.
keyPoints: Capillary plexus beneath dermal papillae is involved in infective vasculitis.
Clinical signs include petechiae, Osler nodes, Janeway lesions, and palpable purpura.
These signs are important clues for diagnosing endocarditis.
items.reference: Infections of the Skin Muscles and Soft Tissues · ANATOMIC RELATIONSHIPS: CLUES TO THE DIAGNOSIS OF SOFT TISSUE INFECTIONS (part 1)

items.subtopic: ANATOMIC RELATIONSHIPS: CLUES TO THE DIAGNOSIS OF SOFT TISSUE INFECTIONS (part 1)
items.title: Vesicle Formation and Differential Diagnosis
items.content: Vesicle formation due to viral infections progresses through stages from macules to vesicles, pustules, and scabs. Varicella presents with a 'dewdrop' appearance on the trunk, extremities, and face, while herpes zoster affects a single dermatome.
keyPoints: Vesicle formation due to viral infections progresses through stages: macules, vesicles, pustules, scabs.
Varicella presents with 'dewdrop' appearance on trunk, extremities, and face.
Herpes zoster affects a single dermatome.
items.reference: Infections of the Skin Muscles and Soft Tissues · ANATOMIC RELATIONSHIPS: CLUES TO THE DIAGNOSIS OF SOFT TISSUE INFECTIONS (part 1)

items.subtopic: ANATOMIC RELATIONSHIPS: CLUES TO THE DIAGNOSIS OF SOFT TISSUE INFECTIONS (part 2)
items.title: Recognizing Rickettsialpox and Blistering Dactylitis
items.content: Rickettsialpox presents as a painless crusted black eschar with an erythematous halo, following mite-bite inoculation of Rickettsia akari. It is important to note its historical prevalence in the northeastern United States and Ukraine, but recent cases have been reported in Ohio, Arizona, and Utah. Blistering dactylitis, on the other hand, manifests as a painful vesicular infection of the distal digits' pulps due to S. aureus or group A streptococcus.
keyPoints: Rickettsialpox: 1- to 2.5-cm painless crusted black eschar with erythematous halo and proximal adenopathy
Recent cases of Rickettsialpox reported in Ohio, Arizona, Utah
Blistering dactylitis: Painful vesicular infection of distal digits' pulps by S. aureus or group A streptococcus
items.reference: Infections of the Skin Muscles and Soft Tissues · ANATOMIC RELATIONSHIPS: CLUES TO THE DIAGNOSIS OF SOFT TISSUE INFECTIONS (part 2) | INFECTIONS ASSOCIATED WITH BULLAE

items.subtopic: INFECTIONS ASSOCIATED WITH BULLAE
items.title: Distinguishing Staphylococcal Scalded-Skin Syndrome from Toxic Epidermal Necrolysis
items.content: Staphylococcal scalded-skin syndrome (SSSS) in neonates is caused by exfoliatin toxin from phage group II S. aureus and must be differentiated from toxic epidermal necrolysis (TEN), which occurs primarily in adults, is drug-induced, and has a higher mortality rate. A punch biopsy with frozen section can help distinguish between the two based on the cleavage plane: stratum corneum in SSSS versus stratum germinativum in TEN.
keyPoints: SSSS caused by exfoliatin toxin from phage group II S. aureus
TEN is drug-induced, occurs primarily in adults, higher mortality rate than SSSS
Punch biopsy with frozen section: Cleavage plane at stratum corneum for SSSS, stratum germinativum for TEN
items.reference: Infections of the Skin Muscles and Soft Tissues · ANATOMIC RELATIONSHIPS: CLUES TO THE DIAGNOSIS OF SOFT TISSUE INFECTIONS (part 2) | INFECTIONS ASSOCIATED WITH BULLAE

items.subtopic: INFECTIONS ASSOCIATED WITH CRUSTED LESIONS | FOLLICULITIS
items.title: Recognizing Impetigo Contagiosa: Key to Preventing Poststreptococcal Glomerulonephritis
items.content: Impetigo contagiosa, primarily caused by S. pyogenes, is prevalent among children aged 2–5 years and can lead to poststreptococcal glomerulonephritis if not identified and managed properly.
keyPoints: Caused by S. pyogenes (impetigo contagiosa) or S. aureus (bullous impetigo).
Common in children aged 2–5 years, especially in poor hygiene settings.
Recognize to prevent poststreptococcal glomerulonephritis.
items.reference: Infections of the Skin Muscles and Soft Tissues · INFECTIONS ASSOCIATED WITH CRUSTED LESIONS | FOLLICULITIS

items.subtopic: INFECTIONS ASSOCIATED WITH CRUSTED LESIONS | FOLLICULITIS
items.title: Diagnosing Hot-Tub Folliculitis: Role of Pseudomonas aeruginosa in Water Temperature and Chlorination
items.content: Hot-tub folliculitis, caused by Pseudomonas aeruginosa, occurs due to insufficient chlorination and water temperatures between 37–40°C. It is usually self-limited but can lead to severe complications like bacteremia and shock.
keyPoints: Caused by Pseudomonas aeruginosa in inadequately chlorinated hot tubs.
Occurs at water temperatures of 37–40°C.
Self-limited, though bacteremia and shock are possible.
items.reference: Infections of the Skin Muscles and Soft Tissues · INFECTIONS ASSOCIATED WITH CRUSTED LESIONS | FOLLICULITIS

items.subtopic: PAPULAR AND NODULAR LESIONS
items.title: Identify the cause of raised nodules post-cosmetic procedures
items.content: Raised erythematous nodules following cosmetic laser surgery or tattooing may be due to Mycobacterium abscessus and M. chelonae infections, respectively.
keyPoints: Mycobacterium abscessus and M. chelonae are implicated in post-procedure nodules
Patients undergoing cosmetic procedures are at risk
items.reference: Infections of the Skin Muscles and Soft Tissues · PAPULAR AND NODULAR LESIONS

items.subtopic: PAPULAR AND NODULAR LESIONS
items.title: Recognize the clinical presentation of Onchocerca volvulus infection
items.content: Nodules caused by Onchocerca volvulus measure 1–10 cm in diameter and are associated with Simulium fly bites, potentially leading to blindness due to microfilariae migration into the eyes.
keyPoints: Onchocerca volvulus nodules range from 1–10 cm
Simulium fly bites are a transmission vector
Microfilariae can migrate to the eyes causing blindness
items.reference: Infections of the Skin Muscles and Soft Tissues · PAPULAR AND NODULAR LESIONS

items.subtopic: ULCERS WITH OR WITHOUT ESCHARS
items.title: Recognizing Cutaneous Anthrax: From Papule to Eschar
items.content: Cutaneous anthrax starts as a pruritic papule that evolves into an ulcer with vesicles and edema, eventually forming an enlarging ulcer with a black eschar. It may mimic other conditions like psoriasis or eczema.
keyPoints: Cutaneous anthrax progresses from a papule to an ulcer with vesicles and edema, then to an enlarging ulcer with a black eschar.
Lesions can resemble psoriasis, eczema, or impetigo.
Buboes are the major cutaneous manifestation of plague; however, ulcers with eschar occur in 25% of cases.
items.reference: Infections of the Skin Muscles and Soft Tissues · ULCERS WITH OR WITHOUT ESCHARS | ERYSIPELAS

items.subtopic: ERYSIPELAS
items.title: Diagnosing Erysipelas: Key Features and Treatment
items.content: Erysipelas is caused by S. pyogenes, presenting with abrupt onset of fiery-red swelling, well-defined indurated margins, rapid progression, and intense pain. Penicillin treatment is effective.
keyPoints: Characterized by abrupt onset of fiery-red swelling with well-defined indurated margins.
Commonly affects infants and elderly adults.
Treatment with penicillin is effective; symptoms like fever and pain may persist initially.
items.reference: Infections of the Skin Muscles and Soft Tissues · ULCERS WITH OR WITHOUT ESCHARS | ERYSIPELAS

items.subtopic: CELLULITIS (part 1)
items.title: Understanding the Etiology and Presentation of Cellulitis
items.content: Cellulitis is an acute inflammatory condition characterized by localized pain, erythema, swelling, and heat. It can be caused by indigenous flora like S. aureus and S. pyogenes or exogenous bacteria. The history, including epidemiologic data, helps identify the etiology. Gram stain and culture are definitive when drainage or an open wound is present; otherwise, cultures are positive in only 20% of cases.
keyPoints: Cellulitis presents with localized pain, erythema, swelling, and heat.
Causes include S. aureus, S. pyogenes, and various exogenous bacteria.
Gram stain and culture provide definitive diagnosis when drainage or an open wound is present; cultures are positive in only 20% of cases without these findings.
items.reference: Infections of the Skin Muscles and Soft Tissues · CELLULITIS (part 1)

items.subtopic: CELLULITIS (part 1)
items.title: Differentiating Purulent from Nonpurulent Cellulitis
items.content: Cellulitis caused by MSSA or MRSA is usually associated with a focal infection and referred to as purulent cellulitis. In contrast, S. pyogenes causes nonpurulent cellulitis, which spreads rapidly, often with lymphangitis and fever.
keyPoints: MSSA/MRSA cellulitis is called purulent and linked to focal infections like furuncles or abscesses.
S. pyogenes causes nonpurulent cellulitis, characterized by rapid spread, lymphangitis, and fever.
items.reference: Infections of the Skin Muscles and Soft Tissues · CELLULITIS (part 1)

items.subtopic: CELLULITIS (part 1)
items.title: Approach to Treatment of Cellulitis Based on Etiology
items.content: Treatment varies based on the causative organism. MRSA is a common cause in both inpatient and outpatient settings, replacing MSSA. For animal bites, amoxicillin-clavulanate or cefoxitin are recommended due to resistance patterns. Pseudomonas aeruginosa requires surgical inspection and drainage if penetrating injury occurs.
keyPoints: MRSA is increasingly common for cellulitis in both inpatient and outpatient settings.
Amoxicillin-clavulanate, ampicillin-sulbactam, or cefoxitin are recommended for animal bites due to resistance patterns.
Pseudomonas aeruginosa requires surgical inspection and drainage if associated with penetrating injury.
items.reference: Infections of the Skin Muscles and Soft Tissues · CELLULITIS (part 1)

items.subtopic: CELLULITIS (part 2) | NECROTIZING FASCIITIS
items.title: Erysipelothrix rhusiopathiae and its antibiotic susceptibility in cellulitis
items.content: Erysipelothrix rhusiopathiae, a gram-positive aerobic rod associated with fish and swine, causes cellulitis primarily among bone renderers and fishmongers. It is susceptible to most β-lactam antibiotics, including penicillin, erythromycin, clindamycin, tetracycline, and cephalosporins, but resistant to sulfonamides, chloramphenicol, and notably vancomycin.
keyPoints: Erysipelothrix rhusiopathiae is associated with fish and domestic swine.
Causes cellulitis primarily in bone renderers and fishmongers.
Susceptible to β-lactam antibiotics (including penicillin), erythromycin, clindamycin, tetracycline, cephalosporins.
Resistant to sulfonamides, chloramphenicol, vancomycin.
items.reference: Infections of the Skin Muscles and Soft Tissues · CELLULITIS (part 2) | NECROTIZING FASCIITIS

items.subtopic: CELLULITIS (part 2) | NECROTIZING FASCIITIS
items.title: Clinical presentation and progression of necrotizing fasciitis
items.content: Necrotizing fasciitis may be caused by group A Streptococcus, mixed aerobic-anaerobic bacteria, or Clostridium perfringens. Early symptoms include pain or unexplained fever, followed by swelling, brawny edema, and tenderness. Progression leads to dark-red induration of the epidermis with bullae filled with blue or purple fluid, eventually causing skin sloughing and systemic toxicity.
keyPoints: Caused by group A Streptococcus, mixed bacteria, or Clostridium perfringens.
Early symptoms: pain, fever, swelling, brawny edema.
Progression: dark-red induration, bullae with blue/purple fluid, skin sloughing.
Systemic toxicity can lead to shock and multiorgan failure.
items.reference: Infections of the Skin Muscles and Soft Tissues · CELLULITIS (part 2) | NECROTIZING FASCIITIS

items.subtopic: CELLULITIS (part 2) | NECROTIZING FASCIITIS
items.title: Diagnosis and management of necrotizing fasciitis
items.content: Prompt surgical exploration is crucial for diagnosing necrotizing fasciitis. Necrotic tissue must be removed, and Gram staining and culture of excised tissue help identify causative organisms such as group A streptococci, mixed bacteria, MRSA, or Clostridium species.
keyPoints: Prompt surgical exploration is essential for diagnosis.
Necrotic tissue removal is necessary.
Gram staining and culture of excised tissue aid in identifying causative organisms.
items.reference: Infections of the Skin Muscles and Soft Tissues · CELLULITIS (part 2) | NECROTIZING FASCIITIS

items.subtopic: MYOSITIS AND MYONECROSIS
items.title: Recognize and Manage Pyomyositis: Key Pathogens and Presentation
items.content: Pyomyositis, often caused by S. aureus, is prevalent in tropical areas and can occur without a known portal of entry. It typically begins at the site of blunt trauma or muscle strain and remains localized unless toxic shock syndrome toxin 1 or certain enterotoxins are involved.
keyPoints: Commonly due to S. aureus
Prevalent in tropical areas
No known portal of entry
Localized infection unless associated with specific toxins
items.reference: Infections of the Skin Muscles and Soft Tissues · MYOSITIS AND MYONECROSIS

items.subtopic: MYOSITIS AND MYONECROSIS
items.title: Identify and Respond to Gas Gangrene: Pathogenesis and Risk Factors
items.content: Gas gangrene usually follows severe penetrating injuries, often caused by clostridial species like C. perfringens. Spontaneous cases can occur in patients with neutropenia or gastrointestinal malignancy due to the oxygen tolerance of C. septicum.
keyPoints: Caused by clostridial species (e.g., C. perfringens, C. septicum)
Follows severe penetrating injuries
Spontaneous cases in neutropenic patients or those with GI malignancy
C. septicum's oxygen tolerance allows for spontaneous infection
items.reference: Infections of the Skin Muscles and Soft Tissues · MYOSITIS AND MYONECROSIS

items.subtopic: DIAGNOSIS
items.title: When to Use Imaging for Soft Tissue Infections
items.content: Imaging modalities like soft tissue radiography, CT, and MRI are crucial when evaluating rapidly progressing lesions or systemic inflammatory response syndrome. These tests help determine the depth of infection and can identify localized abscesses or gas in tissues, although they may not be specific for fulminant infections such as necrotizing fasciitis.
keyPoints: Use imaging for rapidly progressing lesions or evidence of systemic inflammatory response syndrome.
CT and MRI help determine the depth of infection and identify localized abscesses or gas.
Imaging is not specific for fulminant infections like necrotizing fasciitis if no gas is present.
items.reference: Infections of the Skin Muscles and Soft Tissues · DIAGNOSIS

items.subtopic: DIAGNOSIS
items.title: Role of Aspiration and Biopsy in Soft Tissue Infections
items.content: Aspiration of the leading edge or punch biopsy with frozen section can be valuable, especially when imaging results are positive. Frozen sections help distinguish between conditions like SSSS and TEN and are useful for diagnosing necrotizing fasciitis. However, negative results occur in ~80% of cases.
keyPoints: Aspiration and biopsy may aid diagnosis when imaging is positive.
Frozen sections differentiate SSSS from TEN and diagnose necrotizing fasciitis.
~80% of aspiration or biopsy tests yield negative results.
items.reference: Infections of the Skin Muscles and Soft Tissues · DIAGNOSIS

items.subtopic: Infections of the Skin, Muscles, and Soft Tissues
items.title: Management of Furuncles, Carbuncles, and Abscesses
items.content: Treatment strategies for furuncles, carbuncles, and abscesses depend on lesion size and systemic signs. Smaller lesions (<2.5 cm) can be managed with moist heat, while larger ones (4.5 cm of erythema and induration) require surgical drainage and possibly antibiotics if associated with fever, chills, or leukocytosis.
keyPoints: Furuncles <2.5 cm: treated with moist heat
Larger lesions (4.5 cm erythema/induration): require surgical drainage
Systemic symptoms (fever, chills, leukocytosis) necessitate antibiotics
Trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole or clindamycin for 7-10 days improves cure rates and reduces recurrences
items.reference: Infections of the Skin Muscles and Soft Tissues · Infections of the Skin, Muscles, and Soft Tissues

items.subtopic: Infections of the Skin, Muscles, and Soft Tissues
items.title: Approach to Necrotizing Fasciitis and Myositis
items.content: Early surgical exploration is crucial for suspected necrotizing fasciitis or myositis. This includes removing necrotic tissue, reducing compartment pressure, and obtaining samples for culture. Empirical treatment with clindamycin plus a beta-lactam antibiotic is recommended due to its superior efficacy in experimental models.
keyPoints: Early surgical exploration essential
Remove necrotic tissue, reduce pressure, obtain cultures
Clindamycin plus beta-lactam antibiotic treatment recommended
Group A streptococcal and clostridial infections have high mortality (20–50%) with penicillin alone
items.reference: Infections of the Skin Muscles and Soft Tissues · Infections of the Skin, Muscles, and Soft Tissues

items.subtopic: Infections of the Skin, Muscles, and Soft Tissues
items.title: Use of IVIg in Invasive Group A Streptococcal Infections
items.content: IVIg combined with clindamycin can reduce mortality in invasive group A streptococcal infections. Despite initial controversies, recent studies support its use to mitigate circulating toxins. Multiple doses may be necessary due to variable neutralizing activity.
keyPoints: IVIg plus clindamycin reduces 30- and 90-day mortality
Multiple IVIg doses recommended for consistent toxin neutralization
Use early in treatment course
items.reference: Infections of the Skin Muscles and Soft Tissues · Infections of the Skin, Muscles, and Soft Tissues

items.question: Which factor contributes to the rapid dissemination of microbes causing skin and soft tissue infections?
options: Increased use of antibiotics in agriculture
items.explanation: Efficient air travel facilitates the global spread of microbes.
items.reference: Infections of the Skin Muscles and Soft Tissues · Infections of the Skin, Muscles, and Soft Tissues

items.subtopic: Infections of the Skin, Muscles, and Soft Tissues
items.question: What is a common consequence of natural disasters that increases the risk of skin and soft tissue infections?
options: Major skin and soft tissue damage
items.explanation: Natural disasters often cause injuries that predispose individuals to infection.
items.reference: Infections of the Skin Muscles and Soft Tissues · Infections of the Skin, Muscles, and Soft Tissues

items.subtopic: Infections of the Skin, Muscles, and Soft Tissues
items.question: Which scenario can lead to delayed primary treatment for skin and soft tissue infections?
items.explanation: Disasters, whether human-instigated or natural, can delay primary treatment.
items.reference: Infections of the Skin Muscles and Soft Tissues · Infections of the Skin, Muscles, and Soft Tissues

items.subtopic: ANATOMIC RELATIONSHIPS: CLUES TO THE DIAGNOSIS OF SOFT TISSUE INFECTIONS (part 1)
items.question: Which layer of the skin provides a mechanical barrier against infection?
items.explanation: The stratum corneum is responsible for providing the mechanical barrier that protects against infection.
items.reference: Infections of the Skin Muscles and Soft Tissues · ANATOMIC RELATIONSHIPS: CLUES TO THE DIAGNOSIS OF SOFT TISSUE INFECTIONS (part 1)

items.subtopic: ANATOMIC RELATIONSHIPS: CLUES TO THE DIAGNOSIS OF SOFT TISSUE INFECTIONS (part 1)
items.question: What can cause bacteria to penetrate deeper structures of the skin?
items.explanation: Bacteria can penetrate deeper structures when the protective layer, such as the stratum corneum, is disrupted by burns, bites, abrasions, foreign bodies, or other factors.
items.reference: Infections of the Skin Muscles and Soft Tissues · ANATOMIC RELATIONSHIPS: CLUES TO THE DIAGNOSIS OF SOFT TISSUE INFECTIONS (part 1)

items.subtopic: ANATOMIC RELATIONSHIPS: CLUES TO THE DIAGNOSIS OF SOFT TISSUE INFECTIONS (part 1)
items.question: Which clinical signs suggest infective vasculitis of the plexus beneath the dermal papillae?
options: Dewdrop vesicles on trunk and extremities
items.explanation: Infective vasculitis of the plexus beneath the dermal papillae can result in petechiae, Osler nodes, Janeway lesions, and palpable purpura.
items.reference: Infections of the Skin Muscles and Soft Tissues · ANATOMIC RELATIONSHIPS: CLUES TO THE DIAGNOSIS OF SOFT TISSUE INFECTIONS (part 1)

items.subtopic: ANATOMIC RELATIONSHIPS: CLUES TO THE DIAGNOSIS OF SOFT TISSUE INFECTIONS (part 1)
items.question: What is the typical progression of varicella rash?
options: Macules to vesicles to pustules to scabs
Pustules to macules to vesicles to scabs
Vesicles to macules to pustules to scabs
Scabs to pustules to vesicles to macules
items.explanation: The varicella rash progresses from macules to vesicles, then to pustules, and finally to scabs over the course of 1–2 weeks.
items.reference: Infections of the Skin Muscles and Soft Tissues · ANATOMIC RELATIONSHIPS: CLUES TO THE DIAGNOSIS OF SOFT TISSUE INFECTIONS (part 1)

items.subtopic: ANATOMIC RELATIONSHIPS: CLUES TO THE DIAGNOSIS OF SOFT TISSUE INFECTIONS (part 2) | INFECTIONS ASSOCIATED WITH BULLAE
items.question: Which organism is responsible for blistering dactylitis?
options: Staphylococcus aureus or group A streptococcus
items.explanation: Blistering dactylitis is a painful, vesicular infection of the distal digits caused by S. aureus or group A streptococcus.
items.reference: Infections of the Skin Muscles and Soft Tissues · ANATOMIC RELATIONSHIPS: CLUES TO THE DIAGNOSIS OF SOFT TISSUE INFECTIONS (part 2) | INFECTIONS ASSOCIATED WITH BULLAE

items.subtopic: ANATOMIC RELATIONSHIPS: CLUES TO THE DIAGNOSIS OF SOFT TISSUE INFECTIONS (part 2) | INFECTIONS ASSOCIATED WITH BULLAE
items.question: What is the primary distinguishing feature between SSSS and TEN in terms of biopsy findings?
options: Cleavage plane at stratum corneum for both conditions
Cleavage plane at stratum germinativum for both conditions
Cleavage plane at stratum corneum for SSSS, stratum germinativum for TEN
Cleavage plane at stratum germinativum for SSSS, stratum corneum for TEN
items.explanation: The cleavage plane is the stratum corneum in SSSS and the stratum germinativum in TEN.
items.reference: Infections of the Skin Muscles and Soft Tissues · ANATOMIC RELATIONSHIPS: CLUES TO THE DIAGNOSIS OF SOFT TISSUE INFECTIONS (part 2) | INFECTIONS ASSOCIATED WITH BULLAE

items.subtopic: ANATOMIC RELATIONSHIPS: CLUES TO THE DIAGNOSIS OF SOFT TISSUE INFECTIONS (part 2) | INFECTIONS ASSOCIATED WITH BULLAE
items.question: Which organism is highly susceptible to tetracycline and associated with aggressive infections in patients exposed to Gulf of Mexico waters?
items.explanation: Vibrio vulnificus is highly susceptible to tetracycline and associated with aggressive infections in patients exposed to Gulf of Mexico waters.
items.reference: Infections of the Skin Muscles and Soft Tissues · ANATOMIC RELATIONSHIPS: CLUES TO THE DIAGNOSIS OF SOFT TISSUE INFECTIONS (part 2) | INFECTIONS ASSOCIATED WITH BULLAE

items.subtopic: INFECTIONS ASSOCIATED WITH CRUSTED LESIONS | FOLLICULITIS
items.question: Which organism is most commonly associated with bullous impetigo?
items.explanation: Bullous impetigo is caused by S. aureus.
items.reference: Infections of the Skin Muscles and Soft Tissues · INFECTIONS ASSOCIATED WITH CRUSTED LESIONS | FOLLICULITIS

items.subtopic: INFECTIONS ASSOCIATED WITH CRUSTED LESIONS | FOLLICULITIS
items.question: What condition can be a complication of impetigo contagiosa?
items.explanation: Impetigo contagiosa is related to poststreptococcal glomerulonephritis.
items.reference: Infections of the Skin Muscles and Soft Tissues · INFECTIONS ASSOCIATED WITH CRUSTED LESIONS | FOLLICULITIS

items.subtopic: INFECTIONS ASSOCIATED WITH CRUSTED LESIONS | FOLLICULITIS
items.question: Which organism causes hot-tub folliculitis?
items.explanation: Hot-tub folliculitis is caused by Pseudomonas aeruginosa.
items.reference: Infections of the Skin Muscles and Soft Tissues · INFECTIONS ASSOCIATED WITH CRUSTED LESIONS | FOLLICULITIS

items.subtopic: PAPULAR AND NODULAR LESIONS
items.question: Which organism is responsible for causing raised serpiginous or linear eruptions characteristic of cutaneous larva migrans?
items.explanation: Cutaneous larva migrans is caused by the burrowing larvae of dog or cat hookworms, specifically Ancylostoma brazilense.
items.reference: Infections of the Skin Muscles and Soft Tissues · PAPULAR AND NODULAR LESIONS

items.subtopic: PAPULAR AND NODULAR LESIONS
items.question: Which condition can result in blindness due to migration of microfilariae into the eyes?
items.explanation: Blindness can occur in onchocerciasis due to migration of microfilariae into the eyes.
items.reference: Infections of the Skin Muscles and Soft Tissues · PAPULAR AND NODULAR LESIONS

items.subtopic: PAPULAR AND NODULAR LESIONS
items.question: What is a characteristic feature of tertiary syphilis?
items.explanation: Tertiary syphilis is characterized by large nodules or gummas.
items.reference: Infections of the Skin Muscles and Soft Tissues · PAPULAR AND NODULAR LESIONS

items.subtopic: ULCERS WITH OR WITHOUT ESCHARS | ERYSIPELAS
items.question: Which bacterium is responsible for causing erysipelas?
items.explanation: Erysipelas is caused by Streptococcus pyogenes.
items.reference: Infections of the Skin Muscles and Soft Tissues · ULCERS WITH OR WITHOUT ESCHARS | ERYSIPELAS

items.subtopic: ULCERS WITH OR WITHOUT ESCHARS | ERYSIPELAS
items.question: What is a distinctive feature of erysipelas?
options: Flaccid bullae development on the second or third day
Chronic nonhealing ulcers with overlying dirty-gray membrane
items.explanation: A distinctive feature of erysipelas is flaccid bullae that may develop during the second or third day.
items.reference: Infections of the Skin Muscles and Soft Tissues · ULCERS WITH OR WITHOUT ESCHARS | ERYSIPELAS

items.subtopic: ULCERS WITH OR WITHOUT ESCHARS | ERYSIPELAS
items.question: Which condition is characterized by an abrupt onset of fiery-red swelling with well-defined indurated margins?
items.explanation: Erysipelas is characterized by an abrupt onset of fiery-red swelling with well-defined indurated margins.
items.reference: Infections of the Skin Muscles and Soft Tissues · ULCERS WITH OR WITHOUT ESCHARS | ERYSIPELAS

items.subtopic: CELLULITIS (part 1)
items.question: Which organism is commonly associated with cellulitis following cat bites?
items.explanation: Cellulitis associated with cat bites is commonly caused by Pasteurella multocida.
items.reference: Infections of the Skin Muscles and Soft Tissues · CELLULITIS (part 1)

items.subtopic: CELLULITIS (part 1)
items.question: What type of cellulitis is typically caused by S. pyogenes?
items.explanation: Cellulitis due to S. pyogenes is referred to as nonpurulent cellulitis.
items.reference: Infections of the Skin Muscles and Soft Tissues · CELLULITIS (part 1)

items.subtopic: CELLULITIS (part 1)
items.question: Which condition is associated with recurrent streptococcal cellulitis of the lower extremities?
items.explanation: Recurrent streptococcal cellulitis can be associated with chronic venous stasis, saphenous venectomy for coronary artery bypass surgery, and chronic lymphedema.
items.reference: Infections of the Skin Muscles and Soft Tissues · CELLULITIS (part 1)

items.subtopic: CELLULITIS (part 1)
items.question: Which bacteria is known to cause aggressive cellulitis in freshwater-related injuries?
items.explanation: Aeromonas hydrophila causes aggressive cellulitis in tissues surrounding lacerations sustained in freshwater.
items.reference: Infections of the Skin Muscles and Soft Tissues · CELLULITIS (part 1)

items.subtopic: CELLULITIS (part 2) | NECROTIZING FASCIITIS
items.question: Which antibiotic is Erysipelothrix rhusiopathiae resistant to, making it clinically significant for empirical therapy of skin infections?
items.explanation: Erysipelothrix rhusiopathiae is resistant to vancomycin, which is unusual among gram-positive bacteria.
items.reference: Infections of the Skin Muscles and Soft Tissues · CELLULITIS (part 2) | NECROTIZING FASCIITIS

items.subtopic: CELLULITIS (part 2) | NECROTIZING FASCIITIS
items.question: What organism can cause cellulitis or granulomas on skin surfaces exposed to water in aquariums?
items.explanation: M. marinum can cause cellulitis or granulomas on skin surfaces exposed to water in aquariums.
items.reference: Infections of the Skin Muscles and Soft Tissues · CELLULITIS (part 2) | NECROTIZING FASCIITIS

items.subtopic: CELLULITIS (part 2) | NECROTIZING FASCIITIS
items.question: Which bacteria are associated with necrotizing fasciitis that may present without a defined portal of bacterial entry?
items.explanation: Necrotizing fasciitis caused by S. pyogenes (group A Streptococcus) can present without a defined portal of bacterial entry.
items.reference: Infections of the Skin Muscles and Soft Tissues · CELLULITIS (part 2) | NECROTIZING FASCIITIS

items.subtopic: CELLULITIS (part 2) | NECROTIZING FASCIITIS
items.question: What is the best therapy for infections caused by M. marinum?
items.explanation: The best therapies for M. marinum infections are ethambutol and either clarithromycin or azithromycin.
items.reference: Infections of the Skin Muscles and Soft Tissues · CELLULITIS (part 2) | NECROTIZING FASCIITIS

items.subtopic: MYOSITIS AND MYONECROSIS
items.question: Which organism is most commonly associated with pyomyositis in tropical areas?
items.explanation: Pyomyositis is usually due to S. aureus, especially in tropical areas.
items.reference: Infections of the Skin Muscles and Soft Tissues · MYOSITIS AND MYONECROSIS

items.subtopic: MYOSITIS AND MYONECROSIS
items.question: What condition can lead to spontaneous nontraumatic gangrene among patients with neutropenia?
items.explanation: Spontaneous nontraumatic gangrene among patients with neutropenia is caused by several clostridial species, of which C. septicum is the most commonly involved.
items.reference: Infections of the Skin Muscles and Soft Tissues · MYOSITIS AND MYONECROSIS

items.subtopic: MYOSITIS AND MYONECROSIS
items.question: Which type of myonecrosis occurs concomitantly with necrotizing fasciitis in approximately 50% of cases?
items.explanation: Myonecrosis occurs concomitantly with necrotizing fasciitis in ~50% of cases, and both are part of the streptococcal toxic shock syndrome.
items.reference: Infections of the Skin Muscles and Soft Tissues · MYOSITIS AND MYONECROSIS

items.subtopic: DIAGNOSIS
items.question: Which imaging modality is particularly valuable for defining a localized abscess or detecting gas in tissue?
items.explanation: CT and MRI are useful for determining the depth of infection, defining localized abscesses, or detecting gas in tissues.
items.reference: Infections of the Skin Muscles and Soft Tissues · DIAGNOSIS

items.subtopic: DIAGNOSIS
items.question: What is a limitation of soft tissue radiography, CT, and MRI in diagnosing fulminant infections such as necrotizing fasciitis?
options: They may reveal only soft tissue swelling.
They cannot detect systemic inflammatory response syndrome.
They require the use of contrast agents.
items.explanation: These imaging tests may show only soft tissue swelling and are not specific for fulminant infections like necrotizing fasciitis.
items.reference: Infections of the Skin Muscles and Soft Tissues · DIAGNOSIS

items.subtopic: DIAGNOSIS
items.question: What is the approximate percentage of negative results when using aspiration or punch biopsy with frozen section in diagnosing soft tissue infections?
items.explanation: Negative results occur in approximately 80% of cases when using aspiration or punch biopsy with frozen section.
items.reference: Infections of the Skin Muscles and Soft Tissues · DIAGNOSIS

items.subtopic: Infections of the Skin, Muscles, and Soft Tissues
items.question: What is the recommended treatment for furuncles larger than 2.5 cm?
options: Surgical drainage with antibiotic treatment if associated with systemic symptoms.
Immediate administration of intravenous immune globulin (IVIg).
items.explanation: Furuncles larger than 2.5 cm require surgical drainage, and if associated with systemic symptoms like fever or leukocytosis, antibiotic treatment is also needed.
items.reference: Infections of the Skin Muscles and Soft Tissues · Infections of the Skin, Muscles, and Soft Tissues

items.subtopic: Infections of the Skin, Muscles, and Soft Tissues
items.question: Which antibiotics are recommended for treating MRSA localized abscesses?
items.explanation: A 7- to 10-day course of treatment with trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole or clindamycin is associated with higher cure rates and fewer recurrences in MRSA localized abscesses.
items.reference: Infections of the Skin Muscles and Soft Tissues · Infections of the Skin, Muscles, and Soft Tissues

items.subtopic: Infections of the Skin, Muscles, and Soft Tissues
items.question: What is essential in cases of suspected necrotizing fasciitis?
options: Administration of intravenous immune globulin (IVIg) immediately.
Hyperbaric oxygen therapy as the first line of treatment.
items.explanation: Early and aggressive surgical exploration is essential to visualize deep structures, remove necrotic tissue, reduce compartment pressure, and obtain suitable material for cultures.
items.reference: Infections of the Skin Muscles and Soft Tissues · Infections of the Skin, Muscles, and Soft Tissues

items.subtopic: Infections of the Skin, Muscles, and Soft Tissues
items.question: What combination therapy is recommended for invasive group A streptococcal infections?
items.explanation: Current guidelines recommend treatment with clindamycin (or linezolid) plus a β-lactam antibiotic for invasive group A streptococcal infections.
items.reference: Infections of the Skin Muscles and Soft Tissues · Infections of the Skin, Muscles, and Soft Tissues

items.subtopic: Infections of the Skin, Muscles, and Soft Tissues
items.statement: Trauma from combat activities does not predispose individuals to skin infections.
items.explanation: Combat trauma can damage tissues, increasing infection risk.
items.reference: Infections of the Skin Muscles and Soft Tissues · Infections of the Skin, Muscles, and Soft Tissues

items.subtopic: Infections of the Skin, Muscles, and Soft Tissues
items.statement: The frequency of natural disasters has no impact on the severity of skin infections.
items.explanation: Increased natural disasters can lead to more severe skin infections.
items.reference: Infections of the Skin Muscles and Soft Tissues · Infections of the Skin, Muscles, and Soft Tissues

items.subtopic: ANATOMIC RELATIONSHIPS: CLUES TO THE DIAGNOSIS OF SOFT TISSUE INFECTIONS (part 1)
items.statement: Herpes zoster typically affects multiple dermatomes.
items.explanation: Herpes zoster occurs in a single dermatome.
items.reference: Infections of the Skin Muscles and Soft Tissues · ANATOMIC RELATIONSHIPS: CLUES TO THE DIAGNOSIS OF SOFT TISSUE INFECTIONS (part 1)

items.subtopic: ANATOMIC RELATIONSHIPS: CLUES TO THE DIAGNOSIS OF SOFT TISSUE INFECTIONS (part 1)
items.statement: The stratum corneum is devoid of blood vessels.
items.explanation: The epidermis, including the stratum corneum, is indeed devoid of blood vessels.
items.reference: Infections of the Skin Muscles and Soft Tissues · ANATOMIC RELATIONSHIPS: CLUES TO THE DIAGNOSIS OF SOFT TISSUE INFECTIONS (part 1)

items.subtopic: ANATOMIC RELATIONSHIPS: CLUES TO THE DIAGNOSIS OF SOFT TISSUE INFECTIONS (part 2) | INFECTIONS ASSOCIATED WITH BULLAE
items.statement: The case fatality rate of MPXV is higher than that of smallpox.
items.explanation: MPXV has a lower case fatality rate (1–10%) compared to smallpox.
items.reference: Infections of the Skin Muscles and Soft Tissues · ANATOMIC RELATIONSHIPS: CLUES TO THE DIAGNOSIS OF SOFT TISSUE INFECTIONS (part 2) | INFECTIONS ASSOCIATED WITH BULLAE

items.subtopic: ANATOMIC RELATIONSHIPS: CLUES TO THE DIAGNOSIS OF SOFT TISSUE INFECTIONS (part 2) | INFECTIONS ASSOCIATED WITH BULLAE
items.statement: SSSS is caused by a toxin from phage group II S. aureus.
items.explanation: SSSS in neonates is indeed caused by exfoliatin, a toxin from phage group II S. aureus.
items.reference: Infections of the Skin Muscles and Soft Tissues · ANATOMIC RELATIONSHIPS: CLUES TO THE DIAGNOSIS OF SOFT TISSUE INFECTIONS (part 2) | INFECTIONS ASSOCIATED WITH BULLAE

items.subtopic: INFECTIONS ASSOCIATED WITH CRUSTED LESIONS | FOLLICULITIS
items.statement: Rheumatic fever can be a complication of skin infection caused by S. pyogenes.
items.explanation: Rheumatic fever is not a complication of skin infections caused by S. pyogenes.
items.reference: Infections of the Skin Muscles and Soft Tissues · INFECTIONS ASSOCIATED WITH CRUSTED LESIONS | FOLLICULITIS

items.subtopic: INFECTIONS ASSOCIATED WITH CRUSTED LESIONS | FOLLICULITIS
items.statement: Swimmer's itch occurs when human skin is exposed to water infested with freshwater avian schistosomes.
items.explanation: Swimmer's itch results from exposure to water containing freshwater avian schistosomes.
items.reference: Infections of the Skin Muscles and Soft Tissues · INFECTIONS ASSOCIATED WITH CRUSTED LESIONS | FOLLICULITIS

items.subtopic: PAPULAR AND NODULAR LESIONS
items.statement: Verruga peruana is caused by Bartonella bacilliformis and transmitted by the sandfly Phlebotomus.
items.explanation: Bartonella bacilliformis, transmitted by Phlebotomus sandflies, causes verruga peruana.
items.reference: Infections of the Skin Muscles and Soft Tissues · PAPULAR AND NODULAR LESIONS

items.subtopic: PAPULAR AND NODULAR LESIONS
items.statement: Cysticercosis is caused by larvae of Taenia solium and results in multiple erythematous papules.
items.explanation: Cysticercosis causes subcutaneous nodules, not multiple erythematous papules.
items.reference: Infections of the Skin Muscles and Soft Tissues · PAPULAR AND NODULAR LESIONS

items.subtopic: ULCERS WITH OR WITHOUT ESCHARS | ERYSIPELAS
items.statement: Decubitus ulcers are primarily caused by infections.
items.explanation: Decubitus ulcers are due to tissue hypoxemia secondary to pressure-induced vascular insufficiency, not primarily infections.
items.reference: Infections of the Skin Muscles and Soft Tissues · ULCERS WITH OR WITHOUT ESCHARS | ERYSIPELAS

items.subtopic: ULCERS WITH OR WITHOUT ESCHARS | ERYSIPELAS
items.statement: Erysipelas can extend to deeper soft tissues frequently.
items.explanation: Extension of erysipelas to deeper soft tissues is rare.
items.reference: Infections of the Skin Muscles and Soft Tissues · ULCERS WITH OR WITHOUT ESCHARS | ERYSIPELAS

items.subtopic: CELLULITIS (part 1)
items.statement: Gram stain and culture are always definitive for diagnosing cellulitis.
items.explanation: Cultures are positive in only 20% of cases, making Gram stain and culture not always definitive.
items.reference: Infections of the Skin Muscles and Soft Tissues · CELLULITIS (part 1)

items.subtopic: CELLULITIS (part 1)
items.statement: MRSA is replacing MSSA as a cause of cellulitis.
items.explanation: MRSA is rapidly replacing methicillin-sensitive S. aureus (MSSA) in both inpatient and outpatient settings.
items.reference: Infections of the Skin Muscles and Soft Tissues · CELLULITIS (part 1)

items.subtopic: CELLULITIS (part 2) | NECROTIZING FASCIITIS
items.statement: Necrotizing fasciitis caused by S. pyogenes is often associated with gas in deep tissue.
items.explanation: Gas is usually not present when necrotizing fasciitis is caused by S. pyogenes.
items.reference: Infections of the Skin Muscles and Soft Tissues · CELLULITIS (part 2) | NECROTIZING FASCIITIS

items.subtopic: CELLULITIS (part 2) | NECROTIZING FASCIITIS
items.statement: Erysipelothrix rhusiopathiae is susceptible to most β-lactam antibiotics.
items.explanation: Erysipelothrix rhusiopathiae remains susceptible to most β-lactam antibiotics, including penicillin.
items.reference: Infections of the Skin Muscles and Soft Tissues · CELLULITIS (part 2) | NECROTIZING FASCIITIS

items.subtopic: MYOSITIS AND MYONECROSIS
items.statement: Clostridial myonecrosis can occur without any preceding trauma.
items.explanation: Spontaneous nontraumatic gangrene, caused by clostridial species like C. septicum, can occur in patients with certain conditions.
items.reference: Infections of the Skin Muscles and Soft Tissues · MYOSITIS AND MYONECROSIS

items.subtopic: MYOSITIS AND MYONECROSIS
items.statement: Pyomyositis always develops at the site of blunt trauma or muscle strain.
items.explanation: While pyomyositis begins at the site of blunt trauma or muscle strain, it generally has no known portal of entry.
items.reference: Infections of the Skin Muscles and Soft Tissues · MYOSITIS AND MYONECROSIS

items.subtopic: DIAGNOSIS
items.statement: Open surgical inspection is the best way to determine the extent and severity of infection.
items.explanation: Open surgical inspection, with debridement as indicated, provides material for Gram staining and culture, determining the extent and severity of infection.
items.reference: Infections of the Skin Muscles and Soft Tissues · DIAGNOSIS

items.subtopic: DIAGNOSIS
items.statement: Aspiration alone is inferior to injection and aspiration with normal saline in diagnosing soft tissue infections.
items.explanation: There is some evidence that aspiration alone may be superior to injection and aspiration with normal saline.
items.reference: Infections of the Skin Muscles and Soft Tissues · DIAGNOSIS

items.subtopic: Infections of the Skin, Muscles, and Soft Tissues
items.statement: Hyperbaric oxygen treatment is the primary recommended therapy for necrotizing fasciitis.
items.explanation: Surgical debridement is essential in necrotizing fasciitis; hyperbaric oxygen should not delay or replace it.
items.reference: Infections of the Skin Muscles and Soft Tissues · Infections of the Skin, Muscles, and Soft Tissues

items.subtopic: Infections of the Skin, Muscles, and Soft Tissues
items.statement: IVIg has been shown to reduce mortality when combined with clindamycin for invasive group A streptococcal infections.
items.explanation: Recent studies have demonstrated that IVIg, often in combination with clindamycin, significantly reduces 30- and 90-day mortality.
items.reference: Infections of the Skin Muscles and Soft Tissues · Infections of the Skin, Muscles, and Soft Tissues

items.subtopic: Infections of the Skin, Muscles, and Soft Tissues
items.assertion: The severity of some skin and soft tissue infections has increased in modern times.
items.reason: Microbes have acquired genes for virulence factors and antibiotic resistance due to global dissemination via air travel.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason explains the increase in severity.
items.reference: Infections of the Skin Muscles and Soft Tissues · Infections of the Skin, Muscles, and Soft Tissues

items.subtopic: ANATOMIC RELATIONSHIPS: CLUES TO THE DIAGNOSIS OF SOFT TISSUE INFECTIONS (part 1)
items.assertion: Streptococcus pyogenes can cause rapid superficial spread of erysipelas.
items.reason: Bacteria translocate laterally to deeper structures via lymphatics, resulting in erysipelas.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains how Streptococcus pyogenes causes erysipelas.
items.reference: Infections of the Skin Muscles and Soft Tissues · ANATOMIC RELATIONSHIPS: CLUES TO THE DIAGNOSIS OF SOFT TISSUE INFECTIONS (part 1)

items.subtopic: ANATOMIC RELATIONSHIPS: CLUES TO THE DIAGNOSIS OF SOFT TISSUE INFECTIONS (part 1)
items.assertion: Viremia is necessary for the onset of vesicles in varicella.
items.reason: In varicella, viremia precedes the development of a rash that progresses to vesicles.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, with the reason correctly explaining the necessity of viremia for vesicle formation in varicella.
items.reference: Infections of the Skin Muscles and Soft Tissues · ANATOMIC RELATIONSHIPS: CLUES TO THE DIAGNOSIS OF SOFT TISSUE INFECTIONS (part 1)

items.subtopic: ANATOMIC RELATIONSHIPS: CLUES TO THE DIAGNOSIS OF SOFT TISSUE INFECTIONS (part 2) | INFECTIONS ASSOCIATED WITH BULLAE
items.assertion: Most non-African MPXV cases are associated with tourism to endemic African countries.
items.reason: Human-to-human transmission was documented in a British healthcare worker who attended an infected patient from Nigeria.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, but the reason is not the sole explanation for the association.
items.reference: Infections of the Skin Muscles and Soft Tissues · ANATOMIC RELATIONSHIPS: CLUES TO THE DIAGNOSIS OF SOFT TISSUE INFECTIONS (part 2) | INFECTIONS ASSOCIATED WITH BULLAE

items.subtopic: INFECTIONS ASSOCIATED WITH CRUSTED LESIONS | FOLLICULITIS
items.assertion: Primary infections with dimorphic fungi can present as crusted skin lesions.
items.reason: Fungi such as Blastomyces dermatitidis and Sporothrix schenckii initially resemble ringworm.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Infections of the Skin Muscles and Soft Tissues · INFECTIONS ASSOCIATED WITH CRUSTED LESIONS | FOLLICULITIS

items.subtopic: CELLULITIS (part 1)
items.assertion: Cellulitis caused by S. pyogenes is often associated with lymphangitis.
items.reason: S. pyogenes causes a rapidly spreading, diffuse process that frequently includes lymphangitis and fever.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Infections of the Skin Muscles and Soft Tissues · CELLULITIS (part 1)

items.subtopic: CELLULITIS (part 1)
items.assertion: Streptococcus agalactiae primarily causes cellulitis in young adults.
items.reason: S. agalactiae is known to cause cellulitis mainly in elderly patients and those with diabetes mellitus or peripheral vascular disease.
items.explanation: The assertion is false, while the reason correctly identifies the primary affected groups.
items.reference: Infections of the Skin Muscles and Soft Tissues · CELLULITIS (part 1)

items.subtopic: CELLULITIS (part 2) | NECROTIZING FASCIITIS
items.assertion: Necrotizing fasciitis can present with severe pain and fever as early symptoms.
items.reason: Patients often have a nonpenetrating minor trauma that leads to transient bacteremia.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, but the reason is not the correct explanation for the assertion.
items.reference: Infections of the Skin Muscles and Soft Tissues · CELLULITIS (part 2) | NECROTIZING FASCIITIS

items.subtopic: CELLULITIS (part 2) | NECROTIZING FASCIITIS
items.assertion: Fournier gangrene is characterized by massive swelling of the scrotum and penis.
items.reason: It occurs due to necrotizing fasciitis caused by mixed aerobic-anaerobic bacteria.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Infections of the Skin Muscles and Soft Tissues · CELLULITIS (part 2) | NECROTIZING FASCIITIS

items.subtopic: Infections of the Skin, Muscles, and Soft Tissues
items.assertion: Clindamycin is recommended over β-lactam antibiotics for treating necrotizing fasciitis.
items.reason: Experimental models have shown clindamycin's superior efficacy against streptococcal and clostridial infections.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, with the reason correctly explaining why clindamycin is preferred.
items.reference: Infections of the Skin Muscles and Soft Tissues · Infections of the Skin, Muscles, and Soft Tissues

items.subtopic: Infections of the Skin, Muscles, and Soft Tissues
items.assertion: Moist heat application alone is sufficient for treating furuncles larger than 2.5 cm.
items.reason: Furuncles larger than 2.5 cm require surgical drainage if associated with systemic symptoms.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because moist heat alone is not sufficient; the reason correctly states that larger furuncles need more aggressive treatment.
items.reference: Infections of the Skin Muscles and Soft Tissues · Infections of the Skin, Muscles, and Soft Tissues
